raw_case:
Bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts causing cervicogenic headache
Cervical synovial cysts, while uncommon, occur frequently at the C1/2 level. However, bilateral cysts at this level have not been previously reported. We report a 77-year-old man with cervicogenic headache caused by bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts. He presented with occipital pain that was exacerbated when rotating his head to the left. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed bilateral C1/2 facet joint cysts. Dynamic cervical spine radiography showed no instability. Nonsurgical treatment with a neck brace and an analgesic was initiated. One month later, his symptoms had completely resolved and follow-up imaging demonstrated shrinkage of both cysts. Conservative management can be effective for cervical synovial cysts, even bilateral ones, when the cyst does not extend into the spinal canal and neurological symptoms are minimal. Treatment should be individualized based on patient-specific factors and imaging findings.

Introduction

Degenerative intraspinal cysts are benign lesions that can arise from various spinal structures [1,2]. They occur throughout the spine but are most common in the lumbar region [3]. Most occur in older individuals [4,5].Cervical synovial cysts are rare; in a 2012 literature review, only 132 cases were reported [6]. The incidence of cervical synovial cysts is highest at the C1/2 (30.1%) and C7/T1 (38.3%) levels [6]. Their clinical presentation varies widely but typically involves neck pain, radiculopathy, and myelopathy [6,7]. Treatment often involves surgery, although conservative management has been reported [8,9]. The surgical approach is usually determined based on individual patient factors and the presence of associated spinal conditions [6,10].Bilateral cervical synovial cysts are extremely rare—only 3 previous cases have been reported and all were located at C7/T1 [11], [12], [13]. We report a patient with cervicogenic headache caused by bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts who was successfully managed nonsurgically. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first reported instance of bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts.

Case presentation

A 77-year-old male patient presented to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe pain radiating from the posterior neck to the occipital region that began upon awakening. The patient's past medical history included postoperative gallbladder cancer, emphysema, and acute pancreatitis. There was no history of spinal disorders. His neurological examination was normal; however, he exhibited left posterior neck tenderness and experienced exacerbation of occipital pain upon rotating his head to the left.Blood tests revealed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 5080/µL, hemoglobin (Hb) of 12.0 g/dL, and platelet count of 149,000/µL. Liver function tests showed aspartate aminotransferase (AST) at 16 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) at 16 U/L, and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) at 116 U/L. Renal function tests showed creatinine (Cr) at 1.47 mg/dL, and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) at 23.1 mg/dL. Electrolyte levels were within normal limits, with sodium (Na) at 138 mEq/L, potassium (K) at 4.6 mEq/L, and chloride (Cl) at 108 mEq/L. C-reactive protein (CRP) level was 0.95 mg/dL.Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystic lesions with high T2 signal in the bilateral C1/C2 facet joints. The left cyst was larger, measuring 30 mm wide, 15 mm high, and 10 mm thick, while the right cyst measured 14 mm wide, 8 mm high, and 4 mm thick (Fig. 1). Dynamic cervical spine radiography showed no instability (Fig. 2).Due to severe tenderness in the posterior neck region and radiating pain to the occipital area, combined with imaging findings, the patient was diagnosed with cervicogenic headache associated with C1/2 cervical facet joint cysts was diagnosed.As no apparent instability was observed, nonsurgical treatment was initiated with a neck brace and analgesic medication. The patient reported gradual improvement of symptoms shortly after beginning treatment. At the 1-month follow-up, he reported complete resolution of symptoms. Follow-up MRI demonstrated shrinkage of both cystic lesions (Fig. 3).

Discussion

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first reported case of bilateral synovial cysts at the C1/2 level. The patient was diagnosed with cervicogenic headache based on the presence of pain in the upper posterior neck and in the distribution of the occipital nerves, as well as significant tenderness in the affected area. Nonsurgical treatment with a neck brace and an analgesic was successful.A spinal synovial cyst is a fluid-filled sac that forms because of degenerative facet joint changes. These changes are frequently caused by joint instability, trauma, or inflammation [14]. The cyst can cause localized pain, nerve root compression, and sometimes spinal cord dysfunction. MRI typically shows an iso- to hypointense cyst on T1-weighted imaging that exhibits high signal intensity on T2-weighted sequences. Most are located near the facet joint and some may compress neural structures [15].Cervicogenic headache refers to head pain that originates in the neck region. Cervical facet arthropathy and occipital neuralgia are common causes [16]. Occipital neuralgia typically arises from compression of the greater occipital nerve (GON), which originates from the C2 spinal nerve. The long course of the GON, its peculiar anatomy, and its location in a mobile region of the neck make it susceptible to compression. The C1/2 interlaminar space is a common site of compression, with the C2 ganglion being of particular interest owing to its relatively large size and anatomic variations [17]. Our patient was likely experiencing pain not only from facet arthropathy but also from occipital neuralgia. His pain radiated to the occipital region and the left synovial cyst extended to the posterolateral aspect of the facet joint. These findings suggest that compression of the GON may have been causing occipital neuralgia in addition to pain from facet arthropathy.Both cysts were probably degenerative in nature, given the patient's age and the absence of other clear etiologies. While C1/2 facet joint cysts are frequently associated with instability, this is not always the case, as demonstrated by our patient. This observation challenges the conventional understanding of the pathogenesis of these lesions. However, subtle instability not clearly detectable on dynamic radiography may have been present and could have played a role in cyst formation. This underscores the importance of careful long-term follow-up. Additionally, given the patient's advanced age, age-related facet joint degeneration likely played a role. Furthermore, although there was no clear history of trauma, the possibility of a previous minor trauma being involved cannot be ruled out. These factors, along with potential subtle instability, may have contributed to cyst formation, suggesting the complexity of this condition and the need for ongoing monitoring.The treatment approach to cervical synovial cysts varies depending on symptoms and the presence of instability. Conservative management can be effective in carefully selected patients, as in ours. This typically includes placement of a cervical collar, analgesics, and steroids [9,14]. More invasive options include surgical cyst excision, which is preferred when spinal cord compression is present [18].Our patient's experience illustrates that conservative management can be effective, even with bilateral cysts. The use of a cervical collar and analgesics resulted in rapid symptom improvement and eventual cyst regression, as confirmed on follow-up MRI. The absence of synovial cyst extension into the spinal canal and the lack of neurological symptoms were likely factors that contributed to the success of conservative treatment.

Conclusion

This case adds to the limited literature on cervical synovial cysts and demonstrates the potential efficacy of conservative management in cases where the cyst does not extend into the spinal canal, neurological symptoms are minimal, and instability is not present. Furthermore, it suggests that conservative treatment can lead to favorable results, even with bilateral cysts. This emphasizes the importance of a tailored treatment approach based on individual patient factors and imaging findings.

Ethics approval

This study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Nakamura Memorial Hospital (Approval No. 2024081702).

Patient consent

This study was approved by the institutional review board, and informed consent was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 77-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint**: Sudden onset of severe posterior neck pain radiating to the occipital region.
- **History of Present Illness**: Severe neck pain and occipital headache exacerbated by left head rotation.
- **Past Medical History**: Postoperative gallbladder cancer, emphysema, and acute pancreatitis. No history of spinal disorders.
- **Personal History**: None.
- **Family History**: None.
- **Physical Examination**: Normal neurological examination. Left posterior neck tenderness and exacerbated occipital pain upon left head rotation.
- **Ancillary Tests**:
  - Blood Tests: Normal WBC count (5080/µL), Hb (12.0 g/dL), platelets (149,000/µL), normal liver function tests, renal function with Cr 1.47 mg/dL and BUN 23.1 mg/dL, normal electrolytes, and CRP 0.95 mg/dL.
  - MRI: Bilateral C1/2 facet joint cysts. Left cyst 30×15×10 mm, right cyst 14×8×4 mm.
  - Dynamic Cervical Spine Radiography: No evidence of instability.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Cervicogenic Headache**:
   - Included due to occipital headache exacerbated by neck movement and tenderness in the posterior neck. MRI findings of bilateral C1/2 facet joint cysts supported this diagnosis. 
   - Supported further by the left-sided cyst's potential compression of the greater occipital nerve, contributing to occipital neuralgia.

2. **Occipital Neuralgia**:
   - Considered due to pain radiating to the occipital area and the anatomic relationship between the greater occipital nerve and the left-sided cyst. Likely contributing factor alongside facet joint arthropathy.

3. **Neoplastic Process or Inflammatory Conditions**:
   - Excluded as MRI findings were consistent with degenerative synovial cysts without signs of malignancy or inflammation. Patient's age and history of degenerative processes supported a benign etiology.

4. **Spinal Instability**:
   - Excluded as dynamic radiography revealed no evidence of instability and no neurological symptoms were present.

5. **Other Causes of Neck Pain (e.g., Fracture, Myelopathy)**:
   - Excluded due to unremarkable imaging findings and absence of neurologic deficits.

The key clinical reasoning involved recognizing the bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts on MRI as the anatomical cause of cervicogenic headache and occipital neuralgia. Lack of instability or neurological symptoms allowed exclusion of more serious pathology.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts causing cervicogenic headache, likely associated with occipital neuralgia secondary to compression of the greater occipital nerve by the left cyst.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts causing cervicogenic headache.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. So the patient is a 77-year-old male with sudden severe posterior neck pain radiating to the occipital area. The pain gets worse when he turns his head to the left. His medical history includes gallbladder cancer post-op, emphysema, and acute pancreatitis. No spinal issues before. Physical exam shows tenderness on the left posterior neck and increased pain when rotating the head left. Neurological exam is normal. Blood tests are mostly okay except slightly elevated Cr and BUN, maybe some renal issues, but CRP is 0.95, which is a bit high but not super indicative of infection. The MRI shows cysts in the C1/2 facet joints, left bigger than right. Dynamic X-rays show no instability.

First, considering the sudden onset of neck and occipital pain. Common causes in elderly could include cervical spondylosis, disc herniation, or maybe vascular issues like vertebral artery dissection. But he has no history of spinal problems. The pain is worse with head movement, so maybe mechanical? But the MRI found facet joint cysts. Facet joint cysts, especially in the upper cervical spine, can cause compressive symptoms. But his neuro exam is normal, so maybe the cysts are irritating the surrounding structures without compressing nerves.

Wait, C1/C2 is the atlantoaxial joint. Facet cysts here might impinge on the C2 nerve root, which innervates the occipital area. That would explain the occipital headache. The pain upon rotation might be due to the cyst being compressed or causing inflammation when moving. The size of the left cyst is 30mm, which is quite large. These cysts are often associated with degenerative changes. Since there's no instability on X-ray, that rules out atlantoaxial instability as a cause. 

Other possibilities: infection or tumor? But WBC is normal, CRP slightly elevated. His history of gallbladder cancer could raise concern for metastasis, but the MRI shows cysts, which are more likely degenerative. Acute pancreatitis history might not relate here. 

So the main findings are the facet joint cysts at C1/2, especially the large left one. The location correlates with the pain radiation to the occiput (C2 distribution). The physical exam findings of tenderness and pain on rotation support mechanical irritation from the cyst. No neurological deficits since the cyst isn't compressing the spinal cord or nerve roots enough to cause weakness or sensory loss. 

Alternative diagnosis might be occipital neuralgia, but that's usually due to nerve entrapment or irritation, which the cyst could be causing. Another thought: could this be a synovial cyst? Facet joint cysts are often synovial cysts, which are associated with degeneration. Given the patient's age and the presence of emphysema (a possible indicator of systemic connective tissue issues?), maybe degenerative changes are contributing. 

The dynamic radiographs showing no instability suggest that the pain isn't from a unstable joint but rather the cyst itself causing local irritation. The elevated CRP might indicate some inflammation around the cyst, contributing to pain. 

So putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is cervical facet joint cysts at C1/2 causing cervicogenic occipital headache and neck pain, exacerbated by movement. The left cyst, being larger, is the primary culprit here.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s sudden severe posterior neck pain radiating to the occipital region, exacerbated by head rotation, suggests a mechanical or structural etiology localized to the upper cervical spine. The absence of neurological deficits narrows the focus to non-compressive pathologies.  
<step 2> The MRI findings of bilateral C1/2 facet joint cysts, particularly the large left cyst (30×15×10 mm), are critical. These cysts are likely synovial or ganglion cysts arising from degenerative facet joints, common in the elderly.  
<step 3> The occipital radiation aligns with irritation of the C2 nerve root, which innervates the occipital region. Pain exacerbation during left head rotation correlates with mechanical stress on the left cyst, causing local inflammation or capsular stretch.  
<step 4> Dynamic radiography ruling out instability excludes atlantoaxial instability or ligamentous injury as contributors. Normal inflammatory markers (except mild CRP elevation) and lack of fever reduce suspicion for infection or metastatic involvement despite his cancer history.  
<step 5> The absence of spinal cord compression or radiculopathy on neurological exam supports the diagnosis of pain secondary to cyst-induced mechanical irritation rather than direct neural compression.  

### Answer:
Cervical facet joint cysts at C1/2 causing cervicogenic occipital headache and neck pain.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s sudden severe posterior neck pain radiating to the occipital region, exacerbated by head rotation, suggests a mechanical or structural etiology localized to the upper cervical spine. The absence of neurological deficits narrows the focus to non-compressive pathologies.

The MRI findings of bilateral C1/2 facet joint cysts, particularly the large left cyst (30×15×10 mm), are critical. These cysts are likely synovial or ganglion cysts arising from degenerative facet joints, common in the elderly.

The occipital radiation aligns with irritation of the C2 nerve root, which innervates the occipital region. Pain exacerbation during left head rotation correlates with mechanical stress on the left cyst, causing local inflammation or capsular stretch.

Dynamic radiography ruling out instability excludes atlantoaxial instability or ligamentous injury as contributors. Normal inflammatory markers (except mild CRP elevation) and lack of fever reduce suspicion for infection or metastatic involvement despite his cancer history.
The absence of spinal cord compression or radiculopathy on neurological exam supports the diagnosis of pain secondary to cyst-induced mechanical irritation rather than direct neural compression.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Cervicogenic Headache included due to occipital headache exacerbated by neck movement and tenderness in the posterior neck; MRI findings of bilateral C1/2 facet joint cysts supported this diagnosis; left-sided cyst's potential compression of the greater occipital nerve contributed to occipital neuralgia.

Occipital Neuralgia considered due to pain radiating to the occipital area and anatomic relationship between the greater occipital nerve and the left-sided cyst; likely contributing factor alongside facet joint arthropathy.

Neoplastic Process or Inflammatory Conditions excluded as MRI findings were consistent with degenerative synovial cysts without signs of malignancy or inflammation; patient's age and history of degenerative processes supported a benign etiology.
Spinal Instability excluded as dynamic radiography revealed no evidence of instability and no neurological symptoms were present.
Other Causes of Neck Pain (e.g., Fracture, Myelopathy) excluded due to unremarkable imaging findings and absence of neurologic deficits.

Key clinical reasoning involved recognizing the bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts on MRI as the anatomical cause of cervicogenic headache and occipital neuralgia; lack of instability or neurological symptoms allowed exclusion of more serious pathology.
Final diagnosis: Bilateral C1/2 synovial cysts causing cervicogenic headache, likely associated with occipital neuralgia secondary to compression of the greater occipital nerve by the left cyst.
